transcript.transcript: Am part of the IWS Committee to celebrate Idaho Watercolour Society's 50th anniversary. Today's date is October 2nd, 2025. I'm in Boise with Carole HasBrouck Browning to talk about 50 year history of IWS. The oral history is conducted with support of the Idaho Historical Society as part of the America 250 in Idaho Commemoration. The Oral History Project, of which this interview is a part, supports a commemoration mission to advance the ideals of the American Revolution equality, liberty, and justice through meaningful community engagement and recognize, appreciate, and commemorate Idaho's journey in America's legacy. Carole, first let me say thank you so much for your time. This interview will allow you to reflect on and share your memories and experiences about us, about IWS and our 50th anniversary. Through these questions, I hope to gain insights into the interview outcomes which are IWS role in the arts for artists and art organizations, and the history of IWS as we approach its 50th anniversary. I will begin with some general questions to start the conversation, and we can get into more specific topics. Okay. Do I have your permission to record this interview? And can you state your name and spell your first and last name? Yes, you do have my permission. So the spelling of my name is Carol c a r o l. Hasbrouck capital H a s capital B r o u c k C.K. Browning b r o w n I n g. That was a mouthful. So I read your bio and, uh, you have a very interesting family history about how your family arrived in Idaho. Could you, um, tell us something about yourself? If you if you want some history in the family. Um, my family was part of a group of Huguenots who were ushered out of France because of their religious preferences. Uh, to Holland and then from Holland, uh, they were able to get, uh, grants of land through the King of England in Poughkeepsie, New York. So the eight families built homes there and there are on the historic register today. One of the families were the Delanos, of which Franklin Delano Roosevelt is a member. So very long history connection there. My family owned a hotel on the Hudson River, which burned in the late 1800s. Oh, they then decided from being hotel owners they would become teachers, moved to Arkansas, and then took that road out west, like so many did in the late 1800s, early 1900s, moved to Nampa and bought 200 acres and became dairy farmers. Oh, wow. Well, that was a big change. It was. Yeah. Wow. Okay. And then you had mentioned, oh, at one of our other classes, that your parents had a stationery store, or was it a different type of store? No. My father owned, um, an office supply store called Hass's, this, which was his nickname as a kid. It was a combination of like a staples of Barnes and Noble and a hallmark store because we had cards, books, office supplies, art supplies. Mhm. Yes. Wow. Yeah. And I remember that you said, um, that was maybe the start of your art career because you could sit down with paper and pencil and draw. The only way we could get away from the store was to go camping on, um, some weekends in the summer. My mother was not a fisher woman, so she would bring paper, art paper from the store and pull charcoal from the fire and draw while my father fished. Uh, real charcoal. Keeping me quiet. Okay. Well. That's interesting. You have a very long history in America. Yes. Early 1600s. Wow. That's very interesting. Um. Let's see. So you had a very early start in, um, in art, and I know that you were a charter member of IWS. Yes, I was in, um, 1976. I was going to college and had a semester where I couldn't afford tuition. And so my boss said, quit grumbling and just go find something to do. And that Sunday in the newspaper, in the classified ads, was an ad by Ruth Clark for anyone interested in wanting to help, uh, start a watercolor society. So I called her, and the rest is history. Wow. That's amazing. Um, yeah. That's amazing. 50 years with Idaho Watercolor Society. With watercolor. I actually had quite a large gap in between when I graduated and when I joined again after I retired. Um, right. I read that you had moved away from Idaho. Yes. I lived in South Carolina for 20 years. And did your work take you there? Yes. Okay. And what did you do as an occupation?. Uh, I started out at Albertson's, out of college doing package design and illustration for Albertsons corporate offices. Mhm. Worked for them for about 17 years and then moved to South Carolina. Um, to and I got into database marketing. Um, and at the end I was a director for their corporate foundation. Oh, really? Oh, wow. That was a huge responsibility. We would raise and then donate about $5 million a year. Really? Oh, my gosh, that is very big. Hag and Watt, who were beneficiaries with their local company or local groups. The the grocery chain I worked for had stores in North and South Carolina, Georgia and Tennessee. We would donate where we had stores in those markets. Right. Mhm. Um, Albertson's has a long history of being very good to the community. Yes. Um, okay. Let's see. Um, do you feel like the the places that you live impact your painting, that the geography or the types of plants that grow impact what how you paint or what you paint? Absolutely. Not only the environment that you're in, but the people that you interact with. So Southerners were very different than than what I was used to out West. Um, all very interesting. The flora and the fauna. Certainly. And the temperatures were different, but yes, it it does impact what you paint and what colors you use. Right, right. Even how moist your paint, your paints stay. That's true. Okay. Um. Let's see. So I know that you're currently the president. What other volunteer jobs have you done with Idaho Watercolor Society since you've returned? I started out as secretary. Um, I helped run the annual exhibition and workshop early on. Um, I think that's about it. So when did you return to Idaho? 2017. Oh, it's been very recent, then jumped right in to volunteering. I did, we had critiques that I attended when I first moved back, and I was scared to death. I didn't bring any paintings, um, because I knew who Dwight Williams was, and I didn't want him to make fun of my meager artwork. He never did. But I was intimidated by him. Yeah, his name comes up a lot. I'm sure. In in a positive way. So when you returned, you just jumped right into volunteering again. It's it's a wonderful organization with a lot of amazing, talented and interesting people. So if you learn the backgrounds, the history, the the art journey of the people involved that has an impact on on your art as well. Well let's see, I had some questions. When you took over the presidency, what year was that? 2020? Oh, the year of Covid. Wow. Yes. What were some of the challenges that you had to deal with? Actually, I was elected at the end of 2020. So when I took over, Covid was just ending, um, the very first challenge. Well, the first couple challenges were how do you get people back engaged once they've been at home for a year or more? Uh, unable to associate with other artists. Right. So the first thing we did was we held an online art show. Mhm. So people could have a chance to show their art and yet feel comfortable that they. They were safe and and would stay healthy. Right. When we had the annual exhibition the first year. As president, I was elected in September. And in November I got a phone call from one of our members letting me know that the truck that was taking our traveling show up to Moscow, Idaho, had been hit by a pickup and caught fire, and everything in the truck was burned. The truck driver was injured, and unfortunately, the young man that caused the accident was killed. So we lost everything we had in our show, which wasn't nearly as devastating as the lives that were impacted. Absolutely. I often think of that when I'm driving on a two lane road. It's only a yellow stripe that separates us. Exactly. Um, yeah. Mine was one of the paintings that got burned up. Yes. Mine too. So, um, at the time, at the end of 2020, had our membership dwindled? It had. Um, there were we did online. We did zoom critiques. Right. And that kept, kept people, uh, involved. But it was difficult to stay engaged and, um, when there wasn't activities. Yes. To foster creativity. Yeah. I remember people saying they were they couldn't afford to get Covid and they were afraid to be in community with other people. And so you were dealing with a lot of things. In a large part because the members were elderly and Covid could kill them. Yes. Yes. It was it was a difficult thing for our membership because of the age and also then because of the distance. Given Idaho's geography between Idaho Falls, Pocatello, Moscow. Boise. Um. Yeah. Can you say anything more about the geography issue? The as a challenge. It's one of the things that the Idaho Arts Commission had, been positive about,that we have a statewide organization as opposed to regional organization. We do have a statewide organization, and we have regional directors that keep the board informed of their activities. And they they, um, mentor and encourage new members. And they get together when we have annual meetings. Um, and recently, since I've been president, we started having our annual meetings at various locations throughout the state to encourage, uh, and foster more participation outside of the Treasure Valley area. Mhm. Yeah. I think people really enjoy that. It's kind of like an art vacation. That's exactly how people see it. They they have permission to go play for a couple of days. Mhm. . But we do have huge distances in our state. It takes many hours to get from 1 region to the other? Yes. And from the south to the north, I think is probably the most challenging. Mhm. Right. Yes, I think that's true. Well what were some of the rewards, um, that, that you were able to see in your presidency. Well as we began hosting activities, uh, more people joined back again. Uh, more people joined for the first time. So our membership increased significantly. We held several online shows. We held a show last year for signature members. These are people that have, um, reached a certain status in the number of shows and awards they've they've gained. So we recognize them at the Boise Cascade Building, which is where we had our very first show 50 years before. Oh, it's a nice place. It is a nice place. The other thing we did that I think I'm most pleased with is we got together with the Plein air painters of Idaho and offered between the two organizations, a joint membership. And I think that has been very rewarding for both organizations. Oh, it has infused a new life into our organization. That group is just a very enthusiastic group of artists. And they're a little more spontaneous than we are, which I think is good for the IWS members. Mhm. Yeah. Yeah. You know, everybody wants to know months in advance. But for them they just put it out there on Facebook and then it's a happening thing. Exactly. Well what are some of your uh, hopes for IWS in the future. What would you like to see happen? I would love to see us move about the state even more than we're doing now. Um, this is just in the back of my head at the moment. But given that next year, 2026, is our 50th anniversary Idaho Watercolor Society, it is the state's 136th anniversary, and of course, the country's 250th. So I would love to see us do a show. That is themed to the history and heritage of our country, our state and our organization. Mhm. What would that theme be? Anything. Anything patriotic. Anything that it could be very broad. Mhm. Right. Any characteristics that would represent those ideas. Yeah. I think, uh, an organization's ties to the community and the events that are happening in the community keeps you as part of the community as opposed to an isolated group. A couple of other things that are happening now that I have high hopes for, uh, are involvement with the libraries where we do some short, um, demonstrations and classes with, uh, both adults and children. Um. In the libraries in the Treasure Valley. I know they've done some of those things in both Twin Falls and Idaho Falls. Um, we also are about to start, um, a program through the veterans associations where we teach watercolor online to veterans. We have heard veterans tell us that the process of doing art is extremely calming and helpful to their PTSD. Mmhm. Yes, that would be a wonderful way of being part of the community and giving something back that you're good at and share that enthusiasm. Yes. Well, that's come a long way. Carol, congratulations. I'd say that's a pretty successful presidency. I think it's the whole organization that did that together. Mhm. It does take a village, as they say. That absolutely makes things happen. Um, well, tell me something about your journey as an artist. Well, my first journey was, of course, drawing at the campground with my mother when I was young. Um, had a neighbor lady who was in her 80s and, um, very homebound, but she she gathered neighborhood children and taught them art. Um, in pastels with pastels. So that was really probably the second part of my journey. Um, I was awarded a scholarship to the University of Kansas, um, in the summer during my high school years. So that was pretty exciting. You go thinking pretty hot stuff, and you get there and you see kids from all over the country and you realize, nope, I'm just pretty mediocre. Well, but you got the ticket. The ticket to ride it is. It is a good experience to be around all that talent. And they took us to hallmark. So we got to, oh my, the artists and the writers as they worked and created greeting cards. Oh, that probably really cemented the vision of you could be an artist. Yes, absolutely. And then I went back to Boise State and got a degree in graphic design and illustration. Now, did you work as a graphic designer? I did Albertsons. Albertsons hired me right out of college. Um, they were doing a campaign, so they needed a part time artist, and it was 60 hours a week, part time. Really? Yes. Well, when you do when you do marketing campaigns, it's it's hot and heavy because they have such deadlines. Mhm. Um, after that was over, they hired me full time and I did package design for their private label, Janet Lee products. So it was, it was taking art from a two dimension to a three dimensional aspect. And you had to make packages so you could see all what, six sides. Yeah. That was quite a learning experience as well. Yeah. That would be so interesting. I never think about About how that looks and how you have to create really a three dimensional object, something that's ergonomically pleasant to hold. And private label process tries to compete with the most popular and best sellers. Oh yeah. So you look at the best selling packaging and you make it look similar. Yes. Um, well, with so many things that. Well, go ahead, continue with your journey. Okay. So, um, I worked in the corporate office for probably ten years, and then, then they sent me to Southern California to work as a marketing manager and manage the advertising process, uh, for Southern California. I did that for for a few years, and then, um, came back and was recruited and hired by a marketing firm in Greenville, South Carolina. So that was database marketing, which was, again, something entirely new for me. Oh, so you took information about the area that you were in and then marketed to that information? Is that what databases. They give you? A frequent shopper card. And so the company tracks the things that are purchased at each store. And it allows them to, to purchase more effectively. Sure. Right. Based on what people are buying in that store. So you had to do a lot of statistical analysis, I would think. Yeah. Yeah. Kind of the other side of the brain. Yeah, exactly. That makes my head hurt. It was actually very, very interesting. It was kind of new, um, new venture into the field of of data analytics and marketing. Um, yeah. Your, um, your work has taken you far in the field all over the country. Yes. And did you keep painting in that whole time period that you were away? I didn't, I raised two children. Um, I had some pretty demanding jobs that that kept me busy. But as I neared retirement age, um, I realized I needed to have a hobby. And I had the good fortune to meet a gentleman who was the head of the marketing department for Michelin USA. Michelin tires were headquartered in Greenville, South Carolina. Mhm. And so we got together and he was a watercolor artist. Mhm. So he would come every other week and teach me uh watercolor painting again. Um, so that's where I got into portrait painting. And it was, uh, he was an amazing, amazing guy. And of course, the corporate, um. Marketing experiences we had in common, which, which made it kind of kind of fun. Yeah. Yeah, that was quite the teacher. And then when you came to Boise, you fell into Mareth's portrait class? I did, yes, I had heard about that. And uh, was very, very interested in that because I loved painting people. Mhm. Yeah, I do too. Well um, so you've done, I don't know if you did volunteer work when you were working, but so much volunteer work was volunteerism something your family valued. What, what was your motivation to volunteer your time at in IWS? I think it started with my mother. Um, I'm not sure you'd call this voluntary, but I guess it was she. She was a nurse in Connecticut. And when World War Two came around, she volunteered to be an Army nurse. And so she ran a hospital in New Caledonia. And when I was a child, she volunteered for the Red cross. She was, um. Oh, I can't remember all the things that she did. She volunteered at the church. She, um, she was, uh, daughters of the American Revolution. Um, she did she did a lot of things within the community herself. And I think that's where I caught the bug. And Albertsons, as you said, has always been big on that. Right. So, um, I think I continued that bug when I worked for Albertsons. Yeah. Yeah, that that is a big one. Um, how did you startpainting again? How did you manage your time and be able to carve out space to to paint again. Well, I wasn't working so that that helps a lot. Mhm. Um, I did do some grant writing but I could do that any time of the day or night. So that helped. Um, and then I joined. Oh all sorts of classes in South Carolina. Okay. They had a good arts community there and classes here, too. I took from Ann Sorensen, I took from Mareth Curtis Warren. Um, from Linda Aman. Uh, any chance I got to to pick other people's brains and. Write. See how they worked? And now you're teaching? Yes, yes. And that's just. That's pure joy. Yeah. Can you say something more? I can see that there's an evolution about going from artist to teacher. Yes. Um, I taught at Boise State right after I graduated. For a while, I taught drawing at night. They needed somebody part time that they didn't have to pay too much. Okay. Um. And I thoroughly enjoyed that because. Because these were nontraditional students that were taking drawing classes at night. So that was probably the the first bit of teaching that I did. And, um, then when I had my children, uh, and I was working at the hospital, some of the doctors would ask me, well, I understand that you you teach painting, would you teach my children? So I would have the doctor's children come to the house. This was after I had my, um, my second child, and they paid me a dollar a lesson. Mhm. Um. Getting rich slowly. No. Not ever. Um, but yeah, I guess it's always been part of what I done. And I love I love the teaching art and I'm teaching English now to, um, refugees in Boise, which again, is very much of a pleasure. Oh, that has always been fascinating to me. Um, because it's, um, such a connection process and it makes them successful. Oh my goodness, these ladies are amazing. Um, what they go through, what they've done and and the dedication they have to learn English. Mhm. Well, you know, some of your themes I saw in your bio that um, that you're focusing on the aging process, but it seems like when I think back in your work the things that resonate with people are is your social consciousness. I guess I never, that just kind of happened. The things that interest me to paint. Mhm. Um, some of the people at the food pantry where I work at the church that come because they don't have enough to eat or they don't have a place to live. Mhm. Yeah. Yeah. It's, it's our responsibility I think to give back. Yeah. You're going to make me start crying. You're a pretty wonderful person. Oh well thank you. I'll pay you later. You can edit that out. Um, okay. Well. Ah. Do you have any memories of your time with IWS that that you would that stand out in your mind that you'd like to share? I think probably the critiques for me are there's always an aha moment in every critique I go to. And it's not my paintings, it's it's someone who maybe is just starting or someone that has painted for a while, but a seasoned member then gives them a little tip or a thought or a suggestion that that they had not considered. And so there's this. Oh, and sometimes there's a no, I'm not doing that. Well, maybe I'll do that. Yeah, I guess I probably should. Yeah, I think it's the critiques because of the variety of people that bring things and learn. And just then the camaraderie of artists that get together and share their talents. Yeah, I having those opportunities for people to come together like that, that's, uh, a unique opportunity through IWS. It is otherwise, , painting is a pretty much a solo affair. Yes, yes, it's a pretty lonely business if you don't get together with other people and share what you do and what you know and learn from them. Well, I'd like to thank you for all your volunteerism and I mean, pulling IWS up from the bootstraps after Covid. That was pretty challenging. There were a whole bunch of us that worked to do that. Mhm. Absolutely. Yeah. It's a good group of people. Um, well would you like to add anything more to this interview? No, I think that's probably about enough. It's a great organization and I'm hoping that this is, you know, just the beginning of what it does within the state of Idaho. Yes. Me too. It's a great organization. People have really, truly love it. And it's really helped them personally and artistically as an artist. Yes, definitely. Okay. Well, thank you very much for your time, Carol. I sure appreciate it. Oh. You're welcome. Thank you. All right. Take care. Bye bye. Bye.

lines.line: # Introduction and Purpose of the Interview

> The interviewer introduces the context for the oral history interview, explaining its connection to the Idaho Watercolor Society's 50th anniversary and the America 250 commemoration. The purpose is to reflect on the organization's history, its role in the arts, and to gather insights from Carole Hasbrouck Browning. The interviewer also requests permission to record and asks Carole to state and spell her name, setting a formal and respectful tone for the conversation.

metadata.title: Introduction and Purpose of the Interview
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer introduces the context for the oral history interview, explaining its connection to the Idaho Watercolor Society's 50th anniversary and the America 250 commemoration. The purpose is to reflect on the organization's history, its role in the arts, and to gather insights from Carole Hasbrouck Browning. The interviewer also requests permission to record and asks Carole to state and spell her name, setting a formal and respectful tone for the conversation.

lines.line: # Family History and Early Life

> Carole shares her family's deep roots in America, tracing back to Huguenot ancestors who fled France, settled in New York, and eventually moved west to Idaho. She describes the family's transition from hotel owners to teachers and then dairy farmers in Nampa. Carole also recounts her father's office supply store, which provided her early access to art materials, and how family camping trips and her mother's creative use of charcoal sparked her initial interest in art.

metadata.title: Family History and Early Life
metadata.synopsis: Carole shares her family's deep roots in America, tracing back to Huguenot ancestors who fled France, settled in New York, and eventually moved west to Idaho. She describes the family's transition from hotel owners to teachers and then dairy farmers in Nampa. Carole also recounts her father's office supply store, which provided her early access to art materials, and how family camping trips and her mother's creative use of charcoal sparked her initial interest in art.

lines.line: # Early Art Influences and Involvement with AWS

> Carole discusses her early engagement with art, facilitated by her family's business and creative activities. She recounts how she became a charter member of the Idaho Watercolor Society in 1976 after responding to a newspaper ad. Carole also notes a significant gap in her AWS involvement due to life changes, but highlights the formative impact of her early experiences with the organization.

metadata.title: Early Art Influences and Involvement with AWS
metadata.synopsis: Carole discusses her early engagement with art, facilitated by her family's business and creative activities. She recounts how she became a charter member of the Idaho Watercolor Society in 1976 after responding to a newspaper ad. Carole also notes a significant gap in her AWS involvement due to life changes, but highlights the formative impact of her early experiences with the organization.

lines.line: # Career and Life Outside Idaho

> Carole explains her move to South Carolina, where her career evolved from package design and illustration at Albertsons to database marketing and philanthropy. She describes her responsibilities in corporate giving and how the different environments and cultures she encountered influenced her artistic choices, including subject matter and color palette. The discussion also touches on the impact of geography and community on her art.

metadata.title: Career and Life Outside Idaho
metadata.synopsis: Carole explains her move to South Carolina, where her career evolved from package design and illustration at Albertsons to database marketing and philanthropy. She describes her responsibilities in corporate giving and how the different environments and cultures she encountered influenced her artistic choices, including subject matter and color palette. The discussion also touches on the impact of geography and community on her art.

lines.line: # Return to Idaho and Re-engagement with AWS

> After returning to Idaho in 2017, Carole quickly reconnected with the Idaho Watercolor Society, initially participating in critiques and feeling intimidated by established members. She describes the welcoming nature of the organization and how learning about other members' backgrounds and artistic journeys enriched her own experience. Carole's immediate involvement in volunteering reflects her commitment to the society and its community.

metadata.title: Return to Idaho and Re-engagement with AWS
metadata.synopsis: After returning to Idaho in 2017, Carole quickly reconnected with the Idaho Watercolor Society, initially participating in critiques and feeling intimidated by established members. She describes the welcoming nature of the organization and how learning about other members' backgrounds and artistic journeys enriched her own experience. Carole's immediate involvement in volunteering reflects her commitment to the society and its community.

lines.line: # AWS Presidency and Challenges During COVID-19

> Carole recounts her election as AWS president at the end of 2020, just as the COVID-19 pandemic was winding down. She describes the challenges of re-engaging members after prolonged isolation, organizing online art shows, and coping with a tragic accident that destroyed the society's traveling exhibition. The loss of artwork and the impact on members are discussed, as well as the broader difficulties faced by an aging and geographically dispersed membership during the pandemic.

metadata.title: AWS Presidency and Challenges During COVID-19
metadata.synopsis: Carole recounts her election as AWS president at the end of 2020, just as the COVID-19 pandemic was winding down. She describes the challenges of re-engaging members after prolonged isolation, organizing online art shows, and coping with a tragic accident that destroyed the society's traveling exhibition. The loss of artwork and the impact on members are discussed, as well as the broader difficulties faced by an aging and geographically dispersed membership during the pandemic.

lines.line: # Overcoming Challenges and Organizational Growth

> Carole highlights the positive outcomes of her presidency, including increased membership, successful online and in-person exhibitions, and the recognition of signature members. She describes the collaboration with the Plein Air Painters of Idaho, which brought new energy and enthusiasm to AWS. The organization also began rotating annual meetings across the state to foster broader participation, helping to revitalize the society after the challenges of COVID-19.

metadata.title: Overcoming Challenges and Organizational Growth
metadata.synopsis: Carole highlights the positive outcomes of her presidency, including increased membership, successful online and in-person exhibitions, and the recognition of signature members. She describes the collaboration with the Plein Air Painters of Idaho, which brought new energy and enthusiasm to AWS. The organization also began rotating annual meetings across the state to foster broader participation, helping to revitalize the society after the challenges of COVID-19.

lines.line: # Hopes for the Future and Community Engagement

> Carole shares her vision for AWS, including expanding activities throughout Idaho and organizing a themed exhibition to celebrate the state's and nation's anniversaries. She emphasizes the importance of community ties and describes new outreach initiatives, such as art demonstrations in libraries and online watercolor classes for veterans. These programs aim to foster creativity, support mental health, and strengthen the society's connection to the broader community.

metadata.title: Hopes for the Future and Community Engagement
metadata.synopsis: Carole shares her vision for AWS, including expanding activities throughout Idaho and organizing a themed exhibition to celebrate the state's and nation's anniversaries. She emphasizes the importance of community ties and describes new outreach initiatives, such as art demonstrations in libraries and online watercolor classes for veterans. These programs aim to foster creativity, support mental health, and strengthen the society's connection to the broader community.

lines.line: # Personal Artistic Journey and Teaching

> Carole reflects on her artistic development, from childhood drawing sessions to formal education and a career in graphic design and illustration. She describes her work at Albertsons, including package design and marketing management, and her later transition into database marketing. After raising her children and nearing retirement, Carole rekindled her passion for painting, particularly portraiture, with the help of a mentor. She also discusses her involvement in local art classes and her eventual move into teaching.

metadata.title: Personal Artistic Journey and Teaching
metadata.synopsis: Carole reflects on her artistic development, from childhood drawing sessions to formal education and a career in graphic design and illustration. She describes her work at Albertsons, including package design and marketing management, and her later transition into database marketing. After raising her children and nearing retirement, Carole rekindled her passion for painting, particularly portraiture, with the help of a mentor. She also discusses her involvement in local art classes and her eventual move into teaching.

lines.line: # Volunteerism and Balancing Art with Life

> Carole attributes her commitment to volunteerism to her mother's example as a nurse and community leader. She describes how her professional life at Albertsons, which valued community service, reinforced this ethic. After retiring, Carole found more time to paint and participate in art classes, balancing her creative pursuits with grant writing and other volunteer activities. Her story illustrates the interplay between personal values, professional responsibilities, and artistic growth.

metadata.title: Volunteerism and Balancing Art with Life
metadata.synopsis: Carole attributes her commitment to volunteerism to her mother's example as a nurse and community leader. She describes how her professional life at Albertsons, which valued community service, reinforced this ethic. After retiring, Carole found more time to paint and participate in art classes, balancing her creative pursuits with grant writing and other volunteer activities. Her story illustrates the interplay between personal values, professional responsibilities, and artistic growth.

lines.line: # Reflections on Teaching and Social Themes in Art

> Carole discusses her experiences teaching art at Boise State, to children, and later to refugees in Boise. She finds great joy in teaching and values the connections formed with students from diverse backgrounds. The conversation also touches on the social consciousness present in her artwork, inspired by her interactions with people facing hardship, such as those at food pantries. Carole sees art and teaching as ways to give back to the community and address important social issues.

metadata.title: Reflections on Teaching and Social Themes in Art
metadata.synopsis: Carole discusses her experiences teaching art at Boise State, to children, and later to refugees in Boise. She finds great joy in teaching and values the connections formed with students from diverse backgrounds. The conversation also touches on the social consciousness present in her artwork, inspired by her interactions with people facing hardship, such as those at food pantries. Carole sees art and teaching as ways to give back to the community and address important social issues.

lines.line: # Memorable AWS Experiences and Closing Thoughts

> Carole reflects on the significance of AWS critiques, where artists of all levels share insights and learn from one another, fostering camaraderie and artistic growth. She emphasizes the importance of community within the organization and expresses gratitude for the collective effort that helped AWS recover after COVID-19. The interview concludes with mutual appreciation and hope for the society's continued positive impact on its members and the broader Idaho arts community.

The interview concludes with mutual appreciation and hope for the society's continued positive impact on its members and the broader Idaho arts community.

children.text: # Introduction and Purpose of the Interview

> The interviewer introduces the context for the oral history interview, explaining its connection to the Idaho Watercolor Society's 50th anniversary and the America 250 commemoration. The purpose is to reflect on the organization's history, its role in the arts, and to gather insights from Carole Hasbrouck Browning. The interviewer also requests permission to record and asks Carole to state and spell her name, setting a formal and respectful tone for the conversation.
# Family History and Early Life

> Carole shares her family's deep roots in America, tracing back to Huguenot ancestors who fled France, settled in New York, and eventually moved west to Idaho. She describes the family's transition from hotel owners to teachers and then dairy farmers in Nampa. Carole also recounts her father's office supply store, which provided her early access to art materials, and how family camping trips and her mother's creative use of charcoal sparked her initial interest in art.
# Early Art Influences and Involvement with AWS

> Carole discusses her early engagement with art, facilitated by her family's business and creative activities. She recounts how she became a charter member of the Idaho Watercolor Society in 1976 after responding to a newspaper ad. Carole also notes a significant gap in her AWS involvement due to life changes, but highlights the formative impact of her early experiences with the organization.
# Career and Life Outside Idaho

> Carole explains her move to South Carolina, where her career evolved from package design and illustration at Albertsons to database marketing and philanthropy. She describes her responsibilities in corporate giving and how the different environments and cultures she encountered influenced her artistic choices, including subject matter and color palette. The discussion also touches on the impact of geography and community on her art.
# Return to Idaho and Re-engagement with AWS

> After returning to Idaho in 2017, Carole quickly reconnected with the Idaho Watercolor Society, initially participating in critiques and feeling intimidated by established members. She describes the welcoming nature of the organization and how learning about other members' backgrounds and artistic journeys enriched her own experience. Carole's immediate involvement in volunteering reflects her commitment to the society and its community.
# AWS Presidency and Challenges During COVID-19

> Carole recounts her election as AWS president at the end of 2020, just as the COVID-19 pandemic was winding down. She describes the challenges of re-engaging members after prolonged isolation, organizing online art shows, and coping with a tragic accident that destroyed the society's traveling exhibition. The loss of artwork and the impact on members are discussed, as well as the broader difficulties faced by an aging and geographically dispersed membership during the pandemic.
# Overcoming Challenges and Organizational Growth

> Carole highlights the positive outcomes of her presidency, including increased membership, successful online and in-person exhibitions, and the recognition of signature members. She describes the collaboration with the Plein Air Painters of Idaho, which brought new energy and enthusiasm to AWS. The organization also began rotating annual meetings across the state to foster broader participation, helping to revitalize the society after the challenges of COVID-19.
# Hopes for the Future and Community Engagement

> Carole shares her vision for AWS, including expanding activities throughout Idaho and organizing a themed exhibition to celebrate the state's and nation's anniversaries. She emphasizes the importance of community ties and describes new outreach initiatives, such as art demonstrations in libraries and online watercolor classes for veterans. These programs aim to foster creativity, support mental health, and strengthen the society's connection to the broader community.
# Personal Artistic Journey and Teaching

> Carole reflects on her artistic development, from childhood drawing sessions to formal education and a career in graphic design and illustration. She describes her work at Albertsons, including package design and marketing management, and her later transition into database marketing. After raising her children and nearing retirement, Carole rekindled her passion for painting, particularly portraiture, with the help of a mentor. She also discusses her involvement in local art classes and her eventual move into teaching.
# Volunteerism and Balancing Art with Life

> Carole attributes her commitment to volunteerism to her mother's example as a nurse and community leader. She describes how her professional life at Albertsons, which valued community service, reinforced this ethic. After retiring, Carole found more time to paint and participate in art classes, balancing her creative pursuits with grant writing and other volunteer activities. Her story illustrates the interplay between personal values, professional responsibilities, and artistic growth.
# Reflections on Teaching and Social Themes in Art

> Carole discusses her experiences teaching art at Boise State, to children, and later to refugees in Boise. She finds great joy in teaching and values the connections formed with students from diverse backgrounds. The conversation also touches on the social consciousness present in her artwork, inspired by her interactions with people facing hardship, such as those at food pantries. Carole sees art and teaching as ways to give back to the community and address important social issues.
# Memorable AWS Experiences and Closing Thoughts

> Carole reflects on the significance of AWS critiques, where artists of all levels share insights and learn from one another, fostering camaraderie and artistic growth. She emphasizes the importance of community within the organization and expresses gratitude for the collective effort that helped AWS recover after COVID-19. The interview concludes with mutual appreciation and hope for the society's continued positive impact on its members and the broader Idaho arts community.

story.title: IWS 50 Anniversary interview- Carol Hasbrouck Browning
story.description: The interview discusses the history and current efforts of the Idaho Watercolor Society (IWS) as it approaches its 50th anniversary, highlighting Carole HasBrouck Browning's involvement as a charter member, and later as president during challenging times, including the COVID-19 pandemic. Key topics include the role of geography and community in art, and collaborations to engage artists statewide. Browning's personal artistic journey and volunteerism reflect a commitment to mentoring others and fostering community connections. Future hopes for IWS include thematic exhibitions celebrating historical milestones and continued community engagement through educational programs.
